name: gas
context: How gas works on Ethereum and L2s — pricing, estimation, and optimization
info: Gas is the unit of computation on the EVM. Every operation has a fixed gas cost. Transactions specify a gas limit and fee. Understanding gas is essential before deploying contracts or interacting with DeFi.
body:
# Gas on Ethereum

Gas is the unit of computation on the Ethereum Virtual Machine. Every opcode — from adding two numbers (3 gas) to storing a value in contract storage (20,000 gas) — has a fixed gas cost defined in the Yellow Paper.

A transaction's total gas cost = gas used × gas price. The gas price itself has two components since [[ethereum/gas#eip-1559]].

## Why Gas Exists

Gas prevents infinite loops and spam. Without it, anyone could submit a contract that runs forever, halting the network. Gas ensures every computation has a cost, aligning incentives between users and validators.

## Common Gas Costs

| Operation | Gas | Notes |
|-----------|-----|-------|
| ETH transfer | 21,000 | Fixed, simplest tx |
| ERC-20 transfer | ~65,000 | Depends on token contract |
| ERC-20 approve | ~46,000 | One-time per spender |
| Uniswap swap | ~150,000 | Multi-hop swaps cost more |
| Contract deploy | 200k–2M+ | Proportional to bytecode size |
| NFT mint | ~80,000 | Varies by contract |

See [[ethereum/erc20/transfer]] for token transfer specifics, [[ethereum/contracts/deploy]] for deployment optimization.

sections.title: EIP-1559 fee model
sections.body:
Since the London upgrade (Aug 2021), Ethereum uses a dual-fee model:

**Base fee** — Set by the protocol, adjusts up/down by 12.5% per block based on utilization. This ETH is burned, reducing supply.

**Priority fee (tip)** — Optional fee paid directly to the validator. Higher tips = faster inclusion.

Total fee = (base fee + priority fee) × gas used.

The base fee creates a price floor that prevents spam while the burning mechanism makes ETH deflationary during high usage.

```
max_fee = base_fee + max_priority_fee
actual_cost = (base_fee + effective_priority_fee) × gas_used
refund = (max_fee - actual_cost_per_gas) × gas_used
```

sections.title: Gas on L2s
sections.body:
Layer 2 rollups have two cost components:

1. **Execution cost** — Running your transaction on the L2 (very cheap, ~0.001× L1)
2. **Data availability cost** — Posting transaction data to L1 as calldata (the dominant cost)

After EIP-4844 (Dencun upgrade), L2s use blob transactions instead of calldata, reducing costs by 10-100×.

| Chain | Typical transfer cost | Relative to L1 |
|-------|----------------------|----------------|
| Base | $0.001–0.01 | ~100–1000× cheaper |
| Optimism | $0.001–0.01 | ~100–1000× cheaper |
| Arbitrum | $0.005–0.05 | ~50–500× cheaper |
| zkSync | $0.01–0.10 | ~20–200× cheaper |

sections.title: Gas estimation
Gas optimization
sections.body:
Key strategies for reducing gas costs:

- **Pack storage variables** — Solidity packs variables into 32-byte slots. Two `uint128`s in one slot cost less than two `uint256`s in two slots.
- **Use calldata over memory** — `calldata` for read-only function args saves ~600 gas per parameter.
- **Batch operations** — One transaction with N transfers beats N separate transactions.
- **Deploy on L2** — Same contracts, 100-1000× cheaper. See [[ethereum/gas#l2-gas]].
- **Off-peak timing** — Gas prices follow usage patterns. Weekends and early UTC mornings are cheapest.
- **Use EIP-2930 access lists** — Pre-declare storage slots to avoid cold access penalties.

related.note: Token operations are the most common gas consumers
